text.content:
Rulebook p. 41-42

Award XP for defeated foes (a common shorthand is the foe's HP total), identified artifacts (their listed XP value), and referee-discretionary awards for clever play, mission completion, and roleplaying.

Use the Award XP button on the character sheet to grant XP via a GM-only dialog.

Rulebook p. 41-42

Pure Strain Humans and humanoids level up as they accumulate XP. Mutated animals, plants, and robots do not level, but they gain attribute bonuses whenever they would have hit a new level threshold.

Level Thresholds (PSH and Humanoid)
LevelXP Required
10
23,000
36,000
412,000
524,000
648,000
796,000
8200,000
9400,000
101,000,000

Rulebook p. 42

At every level-up (or equivalent milestone for non-leveling types), roll 1d10 on the Attribute Bonus table; that attribute gains +1 up to a natural cap of 21.

See the Roll Tables pack's Experience Attribute Bonus (d10) table for the roll mapping.

Sensors: Standard, infrared, ultraviolet, telescopic Control: Self-controiled, Stage V 1.D., special electronic Construction: 3 meter diameter sphere with 1 meter turret on top. Anti-grav pods move the unit at 96 KPH. Two tentacles 5 meters long can manipulate many devices, and are equipped with 10 meter diameter paralysis fields. Twin tractor/pressor beams can move 500 kilos each at 50 meters. This unit is usually assigned to a supervisory borg or a permanent cybernetic installation. Weaponry includes 3 laser batteries of 5 “‘guns”’ each (range and damage — up to 500 meters, short range, 20 dice (20d6) damage per battery; up to 1000 meters, medium range, 15 dice (15d6) damage; up to 1500 meters, long range, 10 dice (10d6) damage), 2 energy grenade launchers (short/medium/long range is 100/300/500 meters) with 2- 40 (2d20) grenades each, 2 micro-missile jaunchers with 2-40 (2d20) missiles each, and 1 photon grenade launcher (250 meter range) with 1-20 (1020) grenades. 200 hit po/nrs, 100 point energy screen, armor class 1.

Warbot Power Sources: Nuclear plant Sensors: Standard, infrared, ultraviolet at 3 kilometer range Controk: Only by a specific permanent cybernetic installation Construction: Vaguely turtle-shell shaped; 9 meters long, 5 meters wide, 3 meters high. Anti-grav pods move the unit at 150 KPH. Weaponry includes 4 micro missile launchers, 6 torc grenade launchers with 500 meter range and 1-100 (2d10) grenades each, 4 black ray cannons with 200 meter range, 8 batteries of 3 Mark VII blaster rifles each, 25 matter bombs with 1 launcher with 200 meter range, 6 laser batteries of & guns” each (range and damage — up to 750 meters, short, 20 dice (20d6) per battery; up to 1500 meters, medium, 15 dice (15d6); up to 3000 meters, long, 10 dice (10d6) of damage). The unit has energy screens that will take 200 points of damage, defending at armor class 1. The unit itself has 509 hit points, armor class 1.

Death Machine Power Sources: Nuclear plant Sensors: Standard, infrared, ultraviolet at 10 kilometer range Contro!: Only by a specific permanent cybernetic installation

EX Construction: 20 meters long, 9 meters wide, 4 meters high, with many knobby projections all over. Anti-grav pads move the unit at 150 KPH. Its own energy screens take 400 points of damage, at armor de class 1. The unit has 750 hit points, armor class 1. pr

Permanent Cybernetic Installation du Power Sources: One or more full size nuclear power plants ad Sensors: Via various subsidiary units (such as supervisory borgs) or

Control: Authorized Stage V |.D., special electronic Construction: Usually building-sized. These units are designed to w control large installations. They are highly advanced versions of the sp main building computers that are so common, with several organic w brains to complement their electronic circuitry. These units usuaily g control all other robotic units on the installation, and are defended by the various security and military units described above. It is ad extremely difficult to take over one of these units. It can comsp municate with and question think tanks. in

po

Think Tank su Power Sources: One or more full size nuclear power plants of Sensors: Standard, special radio frequency op Control: Authorized Stage V I.D., special electronic Construction: Usually building-sized. These units have only one re purpose — to plan for all possible contingencies and answer wi questions about the information they correlate. They were located ch only in very high-security areas such as space ports, secret military bases, etc. Each is defended by its own permanent cybernetic re installation regardless of the existence of another such installation th which runs the base on which the think tank is located. Persons eq with Stage IV and V 1.D.’s can communicate with (not control) a kil think tank, as can supervisory borgs and permanent cybernetic ar installations. va

As player characters and creatures adventure in a GAMMA WORLD campaign, they will gradually gain a number of experience oints. This is a numerical expression of a being’s knowledge and bility to survive and advance in the environment in which it exists. or non-player characters and creatures the primary indication of xperience is age. The assumption is that to survive for any great ength of time in a hostile environment such as GAMMA WORLD would require both learning the pitfalls and dangers that exist and pplying this knowledge in a successful manner. Experience for most non-player creatures is determined by the referee (usually at andom, although it is recommended that a few highly experienced haracters or creatures be placed to guard particularly valuable rtifacts).

Experience for player-characters is of a similar nature as far as efinition, but must be earned by the players as the game rogresses. All new Characters start the campaign with no xperience. Players receive points for various specified actions uring the game, and these points are added to the player's total. In ddition, a referee may award extra experience for some significant r daring action by an individual.

The main advantage of experience is its effect on combat. Beings with high experience have better odds of success. This results in a piral effect — successful combatants receive experience points which contribute toward making success in combat easier, which gives them more experience points, and so on.

Another advantage of increased experience is the award of dditional points to a character’s basic attributes. This is also a piral process. For example, a player discovers (by use of his ntelligence) the operation of some artifact, receives experience oints equal to the value of the artifact, uses it as a weapon in uccessful combat, gains more experience, acquires a point to one f his basic attributes, which increases his chances to successfully perate more artifacts. and so on.

A final use of experience is as a determining factor in the eferee’s decisions. When all other factors are equal, a character ith high experience will receive a more favorable response than a haracter with low experience.

After each session of play, or other interval specttied by the eferee, such as one game week, experience points are awarded in he following manner: 1) for combat, award of experience points is qual to the number of hit points of any hostile being or creature lled or subdued in combat, 2) for acquiring items of value and rtifacts, a number of experience points is awarded equal to the alue of the item in gold pieces, or for the assigned experience

value of artifacts, and 3) experience is awarded to an individual or an entire party by the referee for outstanding actions. Combat experience is divided equally among all participants. Experience is awarded for valuable items only to the character to whom it is given (that is, if the party finds a blaster and other items, each player gets a share of the finds, but only one player will get the experience for the biaster). Experience is not awarded for items which are bought ~ only for those which are found, stolen, or otherwise acquired during the course of an adventure. It is the referee’s prerogative to raise or lower the number of experience points awarded depending on the circumstances. If, for example, one player sneaks out of the ruins with all of the loot while the rest of his party is being eaten by some fierce creature, he should gain some experience points for the items but not all of them.

The bonuses to combat are not gained automatically, nor are they gained every time experience is awarded. Every time experience is awarded, compare the fota/ number of experience points (past experience plus just-acquired experience) with the table below. Each time a player equals or exceeds one of the numbers listed, a ten-sided die is rotled and the number generated is compared with the EXPERIENCE BONUS MATRIX below.

If a bonus is indicated for a basic attribute that is already 18, no bonus is received (and there is no further roll, either).

Die Roll (d10) Bonus

—_ +1 point to mental strength

+1 point to constitution

+1 point to dexterity

RONn;:

+1 point to charisma

+1 point to physical strength

+1 point to intelligence

~~ +1 point on “to hit” roll in physical combat

9-10 +1 point per die of damage inflicted by non-

energy weapon employed
